posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
• 2009 Cecile Tremblay Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru Les Feusselottes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Chambolle-Musigny 1er Cru (12/29/2018)
    Lovely nose of Asian spice, smoked meats, cherry, plum and earth. It has the generosity, ripeness and flesh of the vintage coupled with great minerally detail. Balance and proportion are excellent and sweet, spherical tannins carry the finish on and on.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked: I opened a ‘14 recently, it was almost completely shut.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: She is such a great producer. We only rarely see these in the market here. When they do show up, they are very expensive. Beautiful note.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Also a major fan; she seems to make some of the best if not the best wine from every vineyard. I once compared her Echezeaux to DRC in three vintages, and in all three cases preferred the Tremblay. They just seemed to have greater depth, complexity and more layered and longer finishes. I would love to see what she could do with a Musigny, not that I could afford to buy it if she did make one.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Here too, but only started with the 2010s so not tried an 09 apart from a few small restaurant pours.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Is winemaking also similar to Jayer?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: I have tasted a couple of Jayers, one of which was corked. Not a big enough sample I am afraid.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
[*]

She is such a great producer. We only rarely see these in the market here. When they do show up, they are very expensive. Beautiful note.

and a passionate and serious winemaker. Brings back happy memories of chattering away with her in French on a visit a few years ago.

I am looking forward to trying my bottle of 09 Mugneret-Gibourg Feusselottes in the future (Panos has that one in safe-keeping for me ).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Also a major fan; she seems to make some of the best if not the best wine from every vineyard. I once compared her Echezeaux to DRC in three vintages, and in all three cases preferred the Tremblay. They just seemed to have greater depth, complexity and more layered and longer finishes. I would love to see what she could do with a Musigny, not that I could afford to buy it if she did make one.

She once told me her dream is to make a Clos Saint-Denis!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Is winemaking also similar to Jayer?

Interpreting that as a strictly technical question, not really. Lots of whole cluster… wooden cuves (Jayer used cement), barrels from Chassin not François Frères, and not 100% new. Of course, one can arrive at the same destination via different routes in winemaking. But I think her distant relation to Henri was something importers would try to highlight back when no one had heard of her.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i have been a big fan of her wines ever since i drank an amazing bottle of the 05 vosne vv, however what is the consensus on the bourgogne rouge? it has been consistently disappointing for me. the last bottle i tasted, the 14 croix blanche, was dreadfully over worked not to mention a poor value for $40 let alone outrageous wine-searcher prices.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Just checked on some pricing. Berry Bros in the UK is offering the 2015 Chapelle for $1272 a bottle. My flabber is officially gasted.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: There’s a few bottles of the Chappells on ws in the 500s

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
Yes, one source for each of these Chapelle’s…2013 @ $550, 2014 @ $550, 2015 @ $1000.
Happy shopping!

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked: Sorry, I have unfortunately never tasted her wines, but the price jump they took, seemingly all of a sudden leapfrogging many top tier producers into the pricing stratosphere, just seems…imteresting.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
i have been a big fan of her wines ever since i drank an amazing bottle of the 05 vosne vv, however what is the consensus on the bourgogne rouge? it has been consistently disappointing for me. the last bottle i tasted, the 14 croix blanche, was dreadfully over worked not to mention a poor value for $40 let alone outrageous wine-searcher prices.

Not liking the wine is fine, but since it fermented in a tank with minimal punching down and pumping over and then matured in barrel without racking until bottling, it is hard to see how it could be described as “over worked”.

But I agree that the pricing of Bourgogne from all the top domaines of the Côte de Nuits on the marketplace today is a bit hard to justify—especially vis a vis the best wines of the Côte Chalonnaise.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: that explains why it was very reductive but not the predominant oak character and the maturity of the grapes. i actually served it blind and nobody guessed pinot let alone burgundy. (amongst other wines open there was a 16 metras beaujolais villages which put it to shame). maybe not a representative bottle?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
i have been a big fan of her wines ever since i drank an amazing bottle of the 05 vosne vv, however what is the consensus on the bourgogne rouge? it has been consistently disappointing for me. the last bottle i tasted, the 14 croix blanche, was dreadfully over worked not to mention a poor value for $40 let alone outrageous wine-searcher prices.

Not liking the wine is fine, but since it fermented in a tank with minimal punching down and pumping over and then matured in barrel without racking until bottling, it is hard to see how it could be described as “over worked”.

But I agree that the pricing of Bourgogne from all the top domaines of the Côte de Nuits on the marketplace today is a bit hard to justify—especially vis a vis the best wines of the Côte Chalonnaise.

Totally agree. Thinking of selling some of my pricier bottles, as I will rarely open a $1000+ bottle.

Do you see a bubble, William?

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: There’s been almost no supply of these wines. That will change soon as a number of collectors will sell.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Inspired me to open this:

Will report back, might be hard to drink sideways.
